content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5660           322-5901        322-4441        322-6444

September 15, 1978

Steve Herum
Assistant
Office of Senator John Garamendi
State Capitol, Room 4057
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Herum:

The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission")
has received your letter dated August 30, 1978, concerning a
possible hearing on Proposition Five to be held by the Senate
Health and Welfare Committee.

As you explain in your letter, the purpose of the hear-
ing will be to educate the public on the policy considerations
of Proposition Five. Opponents and proponents of the pro-
position will be given an opportunity to testify. It is the
intention of all connected with the hearing, Committee members
and staff, to conduct the hearing in a non-adversary and
impartial manner. However, it is possible some of the members
will be asked for their position on the proposition.

You have specifically inquired as to any potential
civil and/or criminal liability staff members may have as a
result of preparing for this hearing.

The Commission has jurisdiction over the Political
Reform Act which regulates the disclosure of campaign contri-
butions and expenditures. Although the Commission has
taken the position that the Legislature can make disclosable
contributions to elections when it uses its resources to
support or oppose candidates or measures,1/ not all legislative
activity should or would be construed as a reportable contribution

1/ This position was asserted in FPPC v. Suitt, filed
on November 15, 1977, in Sacramento County Superior Court.
In a related action, Friends of Tom Suitt v. FPPC, Civ. No.
27025, Superior Court Judge Joseph DeCristoforo on January 5,
1978, issued a memorandum of decision stating that the Cali-
fornia State Legislature was not a "person" under the Political
Reform Act and that, therefore, payment by the Legislature
of salary to an employee for campaign work could not be
considered a reportable contribution. That decision has
been appealed to the Court of Appeal.

Steve Herum
September 15, 1978
Page Two

or expenditure. From the facts you have provided us, it is
the opinion of the staff that the hearings which the Senate
Health and Welfare Committee plans to hold would not result
in activity reportable under the Political Reform Act. The
provision of a forum for both sides of a political issue to
express their opinions is not activity resulting in reportable
campaign activity.

The Commission has no jurisdiction over violations of
statutes regulating use of state funds or resources. There-
fore, any question as to the legality of your activity under
such statutes should be addressed to the Attorney General.

I hope this information is helpful. If you have any
other questions, please feel free to contact me.

Sincerely,

Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

BC:kp

STATE CAPITOL, ROOM 4057
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-2407

31 E. CHANNEL, ROOM 403
STOCKTON, CA 95202
(209) 948-7330

POST OFFICE BOX 93
MOKELUMNE HILL, CA 95245

Senate

California Legislature

JOHN GARAMENDI
SENATOR

ALPINE, AMADOR, CALAVERAS, EL DORADO, MONO, SACRAMENTO,
SAN JOAQUIN, STANISLAUS AND TUOLUMNE COUNTIES

August 30, 1978

Ms. Regina Kutchins
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Ms. Kutchins:

The Senate Health and Welfare Committee intends to hold a
hearing on Proposition Five. The purpose of this hearing is to
educate the public as to the policy considerations of the said
proposition. It is the intention of the Committee staff, as
well as the members of the Committee, to conduct the hearing in
a non-advisory, impartial manner. Essentially, every effort will
be made to give both the opponents and proponents of the propo-
sition equal time to explain their position.

It is anticipated, however, that various members of the
Committee may be asked for their personal opinions concerning the
proposition. In fact, there may be members of the Committee who
openly show their particular bias either for or against the
proposition. Please advise us as to the potential civil and/or
criminal liability to staff members who participate in prepara-
tion for this hearing. Needless to say, the staff will perform
the typical functions, such as preparing, typing, and releasing
information to the press, contacting witnesses, writing a back-
ground report, and preparing questions for the members of the
Committee. I am of the opinion that staff would not be subject
to any civil and/or criminal liability for performing basic
legislative functions relative to informing the public as to
the potential public policy effect of a proposed ballot measure.

SENATE COMMITTEE:
AGRICULTURE AND WATER
RESOURCES
HEALTH AND WELFARE
INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS
NATURAL RESOURCES
AND WILDLIFE
PUBLIC UTILITIES, TRANSIT
AND ENERGY
SELECT COMMITTEE ON CHILDREN
AND YOUTH
SELECT COMMITTEE ON MARITIME
INDUSTRY

CHAIRMAN
SUBCOMMITTEE ON CALIFORNIA'S
FOOD AND AGRICULTURAL
ECONOMY
SUBCOMMITTEE ON SOCIAL
SERVICES AND WELFARE

Ms. Regina Kutchins
August 30, 1978
Page Two


I call your attention to Legislative Counsel Opinion No. 3619
(March 1, 1978). However, it would be of great assistance to
us in this matter to have the opinion of the Fair Political
Practices Commission.

     Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steve Herum

                                        STEVE HERUM
                                        Assistant

SH/py

sections.question: You have specifically inquired as to any potential civil and/or criminal liability staff members may have as a result of preparing for this hearing.
sections.conclusion: From the facts you have provided us, it is the opinion of the staff that the hearings which the Senate Health and Welfare Committee plans to hold would not result in activity reportable under the Political Reform Act. The provision of a forum for both sides of a political issue to express their opinions is not activity resulting in reportable campaign activity.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether staff members of the Senate Health and Welfare Committee would incur civil and/or criminal liability under the Political Reform Act for preparing and conducting a public hearing on Proposition Five designed to educate the public on policy considerations with balanced representation from both proponents and opponents.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that the planned Senate Health and Welfare Committee hearing would not generate reportable campaign activity under the Political Reform Act because providing a balanced forum for both sides to express opinions on a proposition is not reportable campaign activity. The Commission has no jurisdiction over violations of statutes regulating use of state funds, so such questions should be directed to the Attorney General.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated August 30, 1975) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 15, 1978). Date discrepancy noted: letter header shows September 15, 1972 but document ID indicates 1978. OCR quality issues with some text degradation. Incoming request signed by Steve Herum, response signed by Barbara Campbell, FPPC Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have specifically inquired as to any potential civil and/or criminal liability staff members may have as a result of preparing for this hearing.

CONCLUSION: From the facts you have provided us, it is the opinion of the staff that the hearings which the Senate Health and Welfare Committee plans to hold would not result in activity reportable under the Political Reform Act. The provision of a forum for both sides of a political issue to express their opinions is not activity resulting in reportable campaign activity.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to an inquiry from Senator John Garamendi's office regarding potential liability for legislative staff conducting a public hearing on Proposition Five. The FPPC concluded that such hearings would not constitute reportable campaign activity under the Political Reform Act.
